title: System Clipboard Registers
subtitle: {key:"+} and {key:"*} — bridge to the OS clipboard.
summary: "+ is the system clipboard. "* is the X11 selection (Linux) or another alias for the clipboard. Yank into them, paste from them, or set 'clipboard' to make Vim use them by default.

blocks.text: If you want text from Vim to land in your system clipboard — or vice versa — use {key:"}{key:+}. {key:"}{key:+}{key:y}{key:y} yanks a line into the clipboard. {key:"}{key:+}{key:p} pastes from it.

blocks.title: + vs *
blocks.text:
Two clipboard registers exist on Linux/X11:

  • **{key:"}{key:+}** — the **CLIPBOARD** selection. The thing Ctrl-C/Ctrl-V uses in most apps. Use this one by default.
  • **{key:"}{key:*}** — the **PRIMARY** selection (X11). The thing middle-click pastes. Less universal; only matters in X11.

On macOS and Windows, both {key:"}{key:+} and {key:"}{key:*} map to the same system clipboard. On WSL, you may need extra setup (e.g. `xclip`, `win32yank`, or Neovim's clipboard provider).

If Vim was built without `+clipboard`, neither register exists. Check with {key::}{key:v}{key:e}{key:r}{key:s}{key:i}{key:o}{key:n}; look for `+clipboard`. Neovim ships with clipboard support that works wherever a provider exists.

blocks.title: Make Yank/Paste Use the Clipboard by Default
blocks.text:
Add to your config:

```
set clipboard=unnamedplus
```

Now {key:y} writes to {key:"}{key:+} (and {key:"}{key:"}); {key:p} reads from {key:"}{key:+}. No more {key:"}{key:+} prefix. The downside: you lose the ability to yank "just to Vim" without touching the system clipboard. Most people find that's a fine trade.

On macOS without X11, use `clipboard=unnamed` instead (writes to {key:"}{key:*}, which equals the system clipboard there).

blocks.title: {key:"}{key:+} and {key:"}{key:*} are I/O ports
blocks.text: These registers are not memory — they delegate to the OS clipboard provider (X11 selection, Windows clipboard, macOS pasteboard). Reading them invokes a system call; writing them does too. They can be missing entirely on a build without clipboard support. Treat them as protocol endpoints with potential failure modes.

blocks.title: The `'clipboard'` Integration Model
blocks.text:
The clipboard is not a Vim primitive — Vim *delegates* to whatever the OS exposes, through a small abstraction called a clipboard provider. The {key:"}{key:+} and {key:"}{key:*} registers are the user-facing names; underneath, every read or write goes through a platform-specific binding:

  • **Linux / X11.** Two distinct selections exist. `PRIMARY` is the *middle-click* selection, set whenever you highlight text with the mouse — maps to {key:"}{key:*}. `CLIPBOARD` is what Ctrl-C/Ctrl-V apps use — maps to {key:"}{key:+}. Both are owned by the application that last wrote them; if Vim exits, a helper (`xsel`, `xclip`, or the desktop session manager) has to keep the bytes alive.
  • **Linux / Wayland.** Same two selections in concept, accessed via `wl-copy`/`wl-paste`. Neovim's built-in provider auto-detects this; classic Vim usually needs `g:clipboard` configured.
  • **macOS.** One *pasteboard*. Both {key:"}{key:+} and {key:"}{key:*} alias to it. The provider shells out to `pbcopy`/`pbpaste` (or links AppKit directly in some builds).
  • **Windows / WSL.** One Windows clipboard. Native Vim links the Win32 clipboard API directly; WSL builds typically use `win32yank.exe` as a provider bridge.

The `'clipboard'` option layers *integration policy* on top of these registers:

  • `set clipboard=unnamed` — yank/delete/put also touch {key:"}{key:*} (PRIMARY on X11, the pasteboard everywhere else).
  • `set clipboard=unnamedplus` — same idea, routed to {key:"}{key:+} (CLIPBOARD). The pragmatic default on Linux and what most users want.
  • `set clipboard=unnamed,unnamedplus` — write to both; useful on X11 where you want middle-click *and* Ctrl-V to work.
  • Empty (default) — leave the OS clipboard alone; ask for it explicitly with {key:"}{key:+} / {key:"}{key:*}.

Check what Vim found with {key::}{key:v}{key:e}{key:r}{key:s}{key:i}{key:o}{key:n} (look for `+clipboard`) and `:checkhealth` in Neovim for the resolved provider chain.
